As I approached the building, a fleeting sense of
unease brushed against me, like a premonition of
impending trouble. It was a sensation people described
before the onset of something ominous, a momentary
shiver that defied explanation. Pushing aside the
unfounded apprehension, I continued forward. The
bulletproof titanium alloy doors loomed ahead, and with
them stood the same individual I had encountered at the
church.
“Ah, we meet again,” the man greeted, his Irish accent
dancing in the air. He exuded confidence, knowing well
that he held the reins within this building’s walls.
“Well, obviously you invited me,” I replied, my smile
carrying a hint of playful arrogance. I was well aware of
his role in this enigmatic establishment. He wouldn’t dare
to step before me unless he was certain of his position.
After all, he was privy to the truth that remained hidden
from the public eye.
“As you can imagine, sir, we should make our way to
106
Threshold’s Pursuit
the underground meeting area. Terminal #0045 will be
waiting for us,” he informed me, a clear path laid out for
our next steps. His words were punctuated with a touch of
formality as he gestured for me to follow.
I fell in step behind him, my anticipation growing with
each stride. This was my inaugural visit to the oldest of
the strongholds, a place shrouded in both mystery and
significance. “It used to be a hospital,” I remarked with a
smile, memories of the past flooding my thoughts. This
hospital held a special place in my history, but my
companion seemed preoccupied, his attention fixed on
matters that prevented him from responding to my
remark.
We entered the building, greeted by an automated door
that swung open, revealing a passage leading to a circular
staircase. As we descended, my impatience began to tug
at my composure. The atmosphere was charged with a
sense of urgency, and I found myself succumbing to the
impatience of my teenage years. It was a trait I had never
been able to fully shake off, despite my accumulated
years. My reservoir of patience had always been rather
shallow, and this moment was no exception.
My patience dwindled further as the ascent continued,
and the inevitable question slipped from my lips, “Well,
are we there yet?” I couldn’t help but indulge in the
impatience of my teen self. Waiting had never been my
strong suit. The question lingered, unanswered, in the air.
However, before I could voice my query again, my
impatience reached its limit. With a sudden decision, I
seized the handrail and took a leap of faith.
As I somersaulted downwards, my mind raced,
simultaneously calculating the number of floors I was
107
Teerth Sharma
descending. The count was astonishing—each floor
seemed to stretch on forever. The plummet seemed to last
an eternity, roughly ten seconds of sheer free fall. My
mental calculation indicated that the underground
expanse was approximately 490 meters beneath the
surface. Just before impact, I deftly reoriented my body,
landing gracefully on my feet.
“Quite a basement you’ve got here,” I quipped,
addressing the incredible depth of this stronghold. The
Irish companion, too, landed with finesse, a testament to
his own skills.
Our dialogue took an intriguing turn. “So, you’re also
one of us?” I inquired, my curiosity piqued. It seemed that
this man, with his Irish charm and enigmatic demeanor,
was not just an ordinary acquaintance.
He nodded, his gaze steady. “Yes, sire. I am one of the
Outriders,” he admitted, revealing his affiliation with the
group.
His actions led us to a door that resembled a vault,
designed to withstand substantial force—perhaps even a
punch from me, though one strike would likely be its
limit. The door’s resilience served as a reminder of the
security that lay beyond. As it swung open, I stepped into
a room dominated by a seemingly ordinary computer.
My frustration was evident as the words escaped my
lips, a mixture of disbelief and exasperation. “So, I
navigated through the traffic for this?” In most
strongholds, the computer systems were nothing short of
fantasy—state-of-the-art machines with specifications
that would make any avid gamer weep tears of joy. But
the computer before me shattered those expectations. Its
appearance was antiquated, reminiscent of technology
108
Threshold’s Pursuit
from eras long past. Even my old MacBook could likely
outpace it, and the LCD display seemed poised to inflict
ocular distress.
“So, this is Terminal #0045?” I sighed, a sense of
anticlimax washing over me. The incongruity of the
situation wasn’t lost on me—a grand entrance to a room
that contained a relic
“Well, it gets the job done,” the Irish man remarked,
his understanding of my concerns evident. Taking a seat
on the small chair he had pulled from a corner, I
immediately connected to the server. Time was of the
essence, especially before this antiquated display. As I
accessed the server, a white blank screen greeted me, void
of any content.
“Enter password,” an automated voice echoed through
the old speakers.
‘Is God willing to prevent evil, but not able? Then he
is not omnipotent.
Is he able, but not willing? Then he is malevolent.
Is he both able and willing? Then why does evil exist?
Is he neither able nor willing? Then why call him
God?’
I typed the thought, aware that the god I was familiar
with bore little resemblance to benevolence. With the
password entered, I accessed what could be likened to a
typical web meeting.
“It seems that the Pest Manager has graced us with his
presence,” a familiar voice spoke—the head of the
foundation known as ‘POF.’
“Well, I had my reasons to be late, and I am no Pest
Manager. Please refer to me by my code name ‘Enuma,’
or better yet, don’t refer to me at all,” I retorted, my tone
109
Teerth Sharma
aligning with his request.
“Ah, I, for one, believe that wasting our time talking
to you is unnecessary,” the chairman chimed in.
“Then who will provide you with funding?” I taunted,
a smirk tugging at my lips.
“Enough bickering, you two,” a voice laced with
beauty intervened. “Chairman, don’t let him get under
your skin. Enuma, relax,” Šanā’s voice resonated, a
calming presence amidst the banter.
“Well, if you say so, Šanā,” I conceded, recognizing
that even my considerable capabilities paled in
comparison to Šanā’s unparalleled intellect and rhetoric.
“As you all are well aware, POF doesn’t convene
meetings of this magnitude unless the matter at hand is of
utmost importance,” Trítos’s unyielding voice spoke, her
dedication to logic as unwavering as ever.
“Pentarchy must act, or else...” the chairman’s voice
trailed off, hanging in the air with an ominous weight.
“Or else what, old man?” interjected Erbe’s ever-
energetic voice. Despite the gravity of the situation, the
familiarity of this group gathering brought a sense of
comfort. Amidst the uncertainty, there was solace in
knowing that a united front stood to protect the things I
held dear.
“Sixth Calamity Class has been detected to be
reincarnated and could potentially return to our world
very soon,” the chairman’s words landed like a
bombshell, shattering the conversation’s casual facade.
The room fell silent, each person present grappling with
the implications of what had just been revealed.
“Wait, so you’ve brought us together just because a
sixth outrider might be joining the Pentarchy? Should we
110
Threshold’s Pursuit
start calling it the Hexarchy now?” I quipped, attempting
to inject some semblance of humor into the increasingly
dire situation unfolding before us.
“Our mana detectors have undergone a recent upgrade,
allowing them to measure not only the quantity but also
the quality of mana,” the chairman explained in a
deliberate and measured tone.
“I played my part in creating those machines, and
Enuma provided the concept,” Sana added with a sense of
accomplishment.
“The readings indicate a mana source with the quantity
of a soldier-level threat but the quality of a calamity
class,” the chairman continued, his words hanging in the
air like a heavy shroud. “It’s even more intense than what
our sensors detected from the First Hero.”
“If this newly reincarnated individual possesses mana
more powerful than the First Hero, it means they have the
potential to annihilate universes,” Trítos’s voice cut
through the tense silence, amplifying the gravity of the
situation.
“And that’s precisely why we’re all here,” Défteros’s
voice joined the discussion, a voice I hadn’t heard in some
time.
“Indeed,” the chairman affirmed.
“Can we infer from this discussion that the newcomer
has a more potent divine heritage than the First Hero?”
Sana’s voice carried a note of contemplation.
“I’m afraid we have no choice but to reach that
conclusion,” Trítos responded, her tone resolute.
“Could we not simply eliminate the comatose body of
the impending threat?” Défteros suggested, his words
tinged with urgency.
111
Teerth Sharma
“It’s not just a partial reincarnation—it’s a full-body
rebirth,” the chairman explained, the weight of his words
sinking in.
“This is worse than bad,” Erbe’s voice trembled, her
concern palpable.
“I have a proposal,” Sana’s voice cut through the
growing unease, capturing everyone’s attention.
“And what might that be?” I inquired, curiosity
piqued.
“She’s thinking exactly what I’m thinking,” the
chairman interjected, a hint of anticipation in his tone.
“If everyone agrees, that is,” Sana added, her voice
holding a glimmer of hope.
And, almost in unison, the word “yes” echoed
throughout the room, a chorus of agreement that
resonated with urgency.
Then, the chairman’s voice took command, a
crescendo of authority that sent shivers down my spine,
““Enuma—the mightiest of Pentarchy, the cosmic
obliterator, the embodiment of infinite devastation. We,
the Pentarchy, implore you not only to step forth but to
utterly annihilate the emerging hero of the First World.
You are allowed to use 100% of your powers”.
112
Threshold’s Pursuit

21. Unveiling Dominion.title: 21. Unveiling Dominion
21. Unveiling Dominion.text:
(Jerry POV)
The Witch of Annihilation didn’t appear to be
interested in my recent victory, as she refrained from
praising me. We swiftly navigated our way out of the
makeshift battlefield, veering towards the outer path of
the city, now stained with the blood of the fallen. The
witch, however, didn’t spare a glance for them, almost as
if she had long been accustomed to the presence of death,
far preceding my own experiences. Witnessing so many
lifeless bodies was a new and unsettling experience for
me. In my world, it wasn’t an everyday occurrence to
engage in battles against dozens of adversaries single-
handedly in order to rectify a mistake. Usually, to make
amends for my errors, a simple apology sufficed.
“Do not misunderstand, brave hero of the elves.
Vampires like us are not weakened by the sight of blood;
rather, the spectacle of battle invigorates us,” Liza said
suddenly, her steps coming to a halt as she positioned
herself in a corner beneath the watchtower’s shadow. “I’m
not seeking rewards. I’m simply intrigued by what sets
you apart from the crowd that gathers to celebrate this
victory,” I inquired, my attempt to impress her still
bearing no fruits. “Part of the reason is your utilization of
mana, and partly it’s what you accomplished with your
mana,” responded the witch. Her demeanour had shifted
from its usual flirtatious nature to a more serious one,
making her harder to read.
“By my utilization of mana, do you mean the way I
113
Teerth Sharma
conjured fire or electricity?” I inquired; a clear sense of
confusion etched on my face. After all, it was Liza who
had encouraged me to integrate the basics of chemistry
and physics into the world of magic. As I brainstormed
this, Liza’s form began to radiate a familiar ethereal
glow—those intricate shapes that manifested around her
when she manipulated her magic. Witnessing this
phenomenon, even though it had occurred multiple times
before, continued to evoke a sense of wonder. But that
was the nature of this world.
“I hope you can perceive these shapes surrounding
me,” Liza stated, her gaze fixed on me.
“Yes, I do,” I responded softly.
“These shapes are a manifestation of my mana,
constantly analysing the elements present around me.
When activated, they simplify the process of performing
magic, and it took me several centuries to refine them,”
Liza explained, her voice tinged with both frustration and
jealousy.
“I’m still not finding the point,” I admitted, feeling
somewhat foolish.
“You can instantaneously analyse billions upon
billions of moles of atoms around you and then direct your
mana to encompass them, manipulating the mana in such
a way that it influences the atoms as intended,” irritation
flashed in her eyes, and she seemed on the brink of
resorting to the methods favoured by the hounds.
“Well, it’s not quite like that for me. I simply
command my mana to create a potential difference, and it
takes care of the rest autonomously. Or I can just ask it to
conjure up hydrogen, and it obliges. Isn’t that what you
mentioned when you taught me the basics of chemistry? I
114
Threshold’s Pursuit
merely instruct my mana,” I explained, a growing sense
of inadequacy gnawing at me.
“The term ‘basic’ was sarcasm,” the Witch of
Annihilation exclaimed exasperatedly. “In this world,
only a few individuals possess the ability to affect the
physics around them. Most people channel their mana
within themselves and convert it into energy tailored for
specific tasks. What you can do is essentially reshape the
bonds and properties of matter surrounding you. It’s as if
you have dominion over it, and here’s the crucial point:
mana of others doesn’t usually respond to commands like
yours. You’re the sole individual in this world with that
capability.”
“Wait, what?” I exclaimed, utterly shocked by her
revelation.
“Yes, mana doesn’t respond the way you used it today.
Merely stating a command rarely achieves anything here.
Almost everyone in this world, except for a select few,
has to learn countless spells to accomplish what you did,”
Liza explained.
“Are spells not just instructions given in a different
language?” I asked, my curiosity piqued.
“Spells essentially create pathways for mana to follow.
They guide the flow of mana and instruct it on what
actions to take. Your method, though, involves direct
commands without providing any guidance on where or
how to apply force. For instance, you said you needed
hydrogen, and it manifested. That difference in power is
tremendous,” Liza explained, her underlying jealousy
evident. It made sense; after all, she was a witch, and from
my childhood lessons, I knew that witches often had a
short temper.
115
Teerth Sharma
“But how is that even possible?” I inquired, making
sure to convey a sympathetic tone to Liza.
“Well, whatever. It’s not as if that changes anything,”
Liza retorted, her irritation palpable.
“I suppose it must be challenging not to have the
power to manipulate physics through simple commands,”
I commented with a hint of audacity.
“Firstly, I am among the rare few who can reshape the
world around themselves. Secondly, the command power
you possess—I’ve created the ‘Flash Enchanting’
technique, ensuring we’re evenly matched in terms of
speed. And don’t you dare think that I would ever lose to
someone like you,” Liza asserted, her anger radiating in a
way that discouraged any further audacity from me.
“Well, considering you’re THE Witch of
Annihilation,” I remarked, layering my words with
flattery.
“Do you even comprehend what it truly means to be
The Witch of Annihilation?” Liza taunted, her tone
cutting. “No, but I’m eager to learn,” I quickly interjected,
hoping to delve deeper into the concept of titles.
“Before we go into that, I need to address the second
matter. First, the stunt you pulled in broad daylight, but
that is beside the point, the second act of yours is so severe
that if the Holy Empire were to find out about it, I doubt
they’d spare your life,” Liza stated, suddenly recalling her
intention to discuss two topics.
“Well, what is it, madam?” I inquired politely, fully
aware that I had already managed to stir the emotions of
one of the most formidable individuals around—an action
that generally leads to unfavourable outcomes.
“The fact that you raised Death with your commands
116
Threshold’s Pursuit
means you hold dominion over them, or at least a certain
control over death,” Liza said, her gaze fixed on me with
seriousness. This makes you desirable to many wealthy
individuals, particularly the Grand Duke who is aging and
would undoubtedly appreciate meeting someone with the
power to manipulate death,” the witch explained.
“Are you trying to win him over” said a friendly voice.
117
Teerth Sharma

22. Convergence.title: 22. Convergence
22. Convergence.text:
As I turned around, a smile graced my face. Standing
before me was Elfona, the matriarch of the elves and the
one person who didn’t radiate a dark aura. She
approached me, her hand resting gently on my shoulder,
her eyes filled with pride.
“Lord Hero, is she threatening you?” Elfona inquired,
her concern evident in her voice.
“No,” I replied, choosing not to escalate any potential
conflict between the trio. I was well aware of the
complexities of their relationships.
Elfona’s next question, however, cut straight to the
heart of the matter. “She purposely didn’t teach you any
spells, did she?” Elfona asked, pulling me closer to her
side with a gentle yet firm grasp.
“Yeah, so what if I did?” Liza retorted defiantly. In
that moment, a subtle but ominous shift occurred.
Everything around Liza took on a distinct tint of purple,
and my instincts screamed at me to take cover.
Elfona’s response was calm but unwavering. “It’s
criminal,” she declared. Even as we stood on a stone road,
I watched as small plants around us sprang to life, their
roots and stems creeping toward me with an eerie
determination.
Suddenly, with a speed that defied comprehension,
Elfona lunged at Liza, and they both vanished from my
sight. I stood there, frozen, my mind racing with worry
about what might transpire next. Then, in an instant, the
creeping roots that had been inching toward me changed
118
Threshold’s Pursuit
their course. They swiftly encased me in a protective
cocoon-like structure.
Before I could even catch my breath, I heard a
tremendous explosion. The voices around me grew
hushed, and my cocoon of writhing plant life withered
away. As I peered outside, the sun dipped below the
horizon, casting an eerie evening glow over the scene.
Before me stood a defeated-looking Liza and a stern-
faced Elfona. To my surprise, they weren’t fighting
anymore.
However, an overwhelming presence filled the air,
sending shivers down my spine. It was a premonition of
something truly terrifying. While my instincts usually
urged me to hide from Liza, the Witch of Annihilation,
this new sensation rendered me utterly helpless. It
compelled me to accept my fate, to surrender to the
impending doom.
That’s when I saw her – Hecate. She loomed in the
background, radiating an impenetrable black aura that
seemed to seep into the environment. It drained the
surroundings of their color, rendering everything in its
path a lifeless black. Above her head, a golden title
hovered like an angelic halo, but it bore an ominous
message: Night’s Sovereign.
“Lord Hero, do you hold any grudge against Liza?”
Hecate asked in a rather casual tone. As I turned to look
at Liza, her eyes pleaded for my support.
“No, I don’t. Liza didn’t do anything worth fighting
over. She simply shared what she could in the limited time
we had,” I replied, recognizing the urgency of preventing
this confrontation from escalating. A serious clash
between these powerful beings could spell disaster for the
119
Teerth Sharma
entire planet.
“I’m surprised you didn’t cause any property damage,”
Hecate remarked, her laughter suggesting that such events
were a normal occurrence.
“Well, Liza did cast a time-stop spell on the property,
freezing everything in its original state until her mana runs
out,” Elfona explained, her voice strangely calm amidst
the chaos.
“Yeah, I’m that good,” Liza chimed in with her usual
flirty tone.
“So, you guys are just friends again?” I asked,
bewildered by the sudden shift from a heated battle to
apparent camaraderie.
“This does feel a bit disorienting,” Hecate admitted
with a smile directed at Elfona.
“What?” I inquired, sensing that I might be the target
of an inside joke.
“Well, the difference in personalities between you and
Lord First,” Liza explained. “While you’re still pondering
over why the fight happened and all, he would be here,
laughing with us. He really knew when to think and when
to just enjoy the moment.”
“I’ve heard a lot about Mister Lord First, but no one
ever told me about his powers,” I remarked, recognizing
the sensitivity of the topic.
The mood grew somber, and silence hung in the air.
Even the stone-paved road seemed to lose its vitality.
“His ability was destruction, and his patron was called
the Chain Archangel, the Left Arm of God,” Elfona
finally broke the silence, her voice barely audible.
“Yes, I know that. The hound I fought told me. But
why is he revered almost as much as a god?” I asked
120
Threshold’s Pursuit
cautiously, treading carefully to avoid offending their
beliefs.
“He was summoned for the first time to defeat the
Titans, primordial consciousnesses created by gods who
absorbed mana and the elements of nature. Leading them
was the Titan of Space-Time, believed to have direct
control over time and space. Somehow, Lord First
confronted and defeated him in his own palace on the
second day of his existence in this world. On his third and
final day of the first trip, he liberated the elves from the
chambers of the Titans, where they were nothing more
than toys. He set the elves free to exist in harmony with
nature, for better or worse,” Liza explained.
“So, he’s the reason the Elven Kingdom started,” I
noted.
“He was summoned again by the fourth Hero in a
campaign to vanquish lesser gods – beings who had
absorbed the remnants of Titans and had the power to
overpower even hundreds of vampires. It was during this
journey that he realized the true nature of God, the entity
experimenting with elements in this universe like it’s a
playground. He promptly established this kingdom and
utilized his power to create Titles, preventing God from
toying with lives. Though when he helped liberate
Vampire nobility from lesser gods and helped them and
elves come together and set up this nation he was called
biased by humans and dubbed ‘The Wicked One’ by the
Holy Kingdom, he cared little for his image. He was truly
the ultimate Hero,” Hecate explained, her voice filled
with reverence and tears glistening in her eyes.
“And he was my husband,” she added, her voice barely
audible, as if meant for my ears alone.
121
Teerth Sharma
“At the end of his last journey, he was bestowed with
the title of the strongest in the universe. So, I hope you’ll
continue his legacy. After all, you come from the same
native place,” Liza said, trying to lighten the atmosphere
with her playful tone, despite the weight of the moment.
“I have the ability to summon two Hero Aids from my
world. Maybe we can use one to summon Lord First?” I
suggested. However, my proposal seemed to have left
everyone momentarily stunned, as if a flashbang had gone
off in their minds, rendering them momentarily
speechless.
Before long, a voice cut through the perplexed silence.
“So, what’s going on here?”
122
Threshold’s Pursuit

23. Resurrection Of.title: 23. Resurrection Of
23. Resurrection Of.text:
From the shadows emerged Lord Koss, exuding his
vampiric allure. “Ah, it seems my keen ears were correct
about the summoning of our Lord,” he remarked, offering
a playful wink as he materialized before me. He
positioned himself squarely in front of the trio.
“However,” he began, “the logistics involved in this
endeavour will be nothing short of mind-boggling. The
impending war will rage from three sides until Our
Benevolent Majesty can manifest in this world. His very
mana will inevitably breach the limitations imposed by
the three-nation pact. I am confident that victory shall be
ours once His Majesty graces us with his presence. But,
let us not underestimate the damage the Holy Kingdom
might inflict in the meantime.”
Liza’s voice resonated with newfound hope as she
interjected, “Nevertheless, there remains a chance to
bring my teacher back, and I am willing to seize it.”
Elfona, seemingly taking a calculated risk, chimed in
with a measured tone, “The details of logistics can be
addressed later. I don’t believe the Holy Empire will
launch their attack with such swiftness.”
“Well, Koss, if you have such a vivid grasp of world
politics, then go and prepare all the borders for war and
alert all the left vampires that ‘we must prepare for an all-
out battle because this world is not letting us resummon
our lord,’” Hecate exclaimed with an excited tone. Her
voice was filled with anticipation as she continued, “This
will certainly ignite their spirits.”
123
Teerth Sharma
“As you say, ma’am,” Lord Koss responded
obediently. With that, he transformed into a swirling
vortex of darkness and disappeared.
Liza then took charge, declaring, “Then we must make
our way to the temple.” She began to levitate, but Hecate
playfully teased her, “Hold on, our little hero can’t fly, so
let’s at least escort him a little so that he can walk there.”
She pointed towards a gate in the wall, and we all walked
towards it. I couldn’t help but wonder if we had enough
time before dawn.
Before I could voice my thoughts, Hecate addressed
them, saying, “No, we vampires don’t melt in the sun, and
what strange ideas you have about us, you weirdo.” She
continued with a smirk, “I can read your mind, you
dumbo.”
Elfona, who had been the most welcoming and kind-
hearted of the trio, chimed in, “Well, let’s climb up these
ladders, Lord Hero, and then we’ll get to see the beautiful
temple.” Her smile was genuine, and it was clear that she
held me in high regard, just as my title suggested.
As we ascended those ladders, I gazed upon the vast
desert stretching across the land, creating a stark contrast
to the city nestled like an oasis amidst the arid expanse.
“We are surrounded by a rainforest, plains, and even a
desert; this location is unique in that regard,” remarked
Liza, as if she were effortlessly reading my thoughts, akin
to a child’s play.
Curiosity tugging at my mind, I inquired, “But how did
this come to be? It seems so unnatural.”
“Well,” Elfona began, her tone tinged with an unusual
sadness, “Lord Hero actually created this desert while
battling the Prince of All Titans. This desert is brimming
124
Threshold’s Pursuit
with time anomalies due to that epic clash. The temple
we’re headed to is where his head landed, which is why
this desert is saturated with mana. However, all the
unnatural wonders you admire right now, they’re not
benign; they’re detrimental to the actual biosphere.”
Understanding washed over me, and I nodded. “I see.”
My gaze remained fixed on the desert, searching for any
signs of peculiarity.
With a swift departure, Liza and Hecate ascended into
the air, vanishing in a burst of sound. Elfona’s demeanour
seemed to shift; her voice grew bolder, and her
countenance transformed. Her once soft opal face now
bore a scowl of anger. “Well, unlike those vampires, we
Elves prioritize style and comfort.”
“Let’s proceed then,” Elfona declared, leaping from
the wall. “Follow me, Lord Hero,” she beckoned from
below. I hesitated, nerves bubbling within, but my trust in
Elfona, perhaps due to her kind nature or her profound
respect for me, urged me forward. With closed eyes, I
took a deep breath and made the leap.
As I opened my eyes, I found myself cradled in a soft
embrace, nestled on Elfona’s thigh. Her words carried a
sense of urgency, “Lord Hero, you must grow stronger,
especially with the return of the FIRST hero.” As she rose,
it became apparent that we weren’t moving
conventionally; rather, it felt like the world around us was
in motion. Elfona was manipulating space, guiding us as
if we were riding a cosmic wave. “If you’re wondering
about our mode of transportation, I’m simply navigating
us through space, like we’re some kind of wave,” she
explained with sincerity. “Don’t inquire about the
specifics; Liza is more knowledgeable in that area. I
125
Teerth Sharma
apologize for her behaviours; her species tends to drain
her kindness.”
As we approached the temple, it stood as a testament
to a bygone era. This temple, constructed after the demise
of the Prince of All Titans, was a haunting marvel. It was
crafted from dark, weathered stone, reminiscent of
ancient Gothic architecture. The structure was surrounded
by a serene pond, mirroring the temple’s imposing
presence. The temple’s design bore a striking
resemblance to the architectural grandeur of an old
mosque, with intricately carved arches and towering
minaret-like spires.
“Behold the temple of destruction etched upon the
mana-filled area where the Titan of Time’s head landed.
This will be where our Lord will return,” announced
Hecate, her voice resonating with a sense of solemnity as
she approached me. “I will protect you with my life, while
Liza will protect the universe from destruction, and
Elfona will safeguard the area from collapsing,” she
declared, joined by Liza, whose request for action carried
a rare note of genuineness.
With a sense of purpose, I turned my back to the
temple, determined not to harm its sacred structure. “Start
the summoning!” I shouted, echoing my previous
experience. My vision glitched, and I found myself back
in my system viewer, my access window to the Index of
Magic. My health was fully restored, and my mana
reserves were replenished, although they fluctuated
strangely, possibly due to my unique title, ‘Judgment of
God,’ continuously filling them. I focused harder,
recalling everything I knew about the First Hero.
Slowly, a blue vortex materialized before me, but
126
Threshold’s Pursuit
instead of sucking in the air, it expelled it outward, as if it
were meant to expel something rather than draw it in. the
summoning had began.
127
Teerth Sharma

24. Revelation.title: 24. Revelation
24. Revelation.text:
(Charlie POV)
“This pentarchy gives me a headache, really,” I
complained, though I knew it was a futile protest. The
decision had been made collectively by the pentarchy, and
opposing them would mean exposing my privacy and
potentially risking a battle against all five of them and
their respective armies from every nation.
“Well, it can’t be helped, sir. The Sixth Calamity Class
is a major obstacle when it comes to decision-making.
The efficiency of the pentarchy hinges on their unique
personalities,” the Irishman said, attempting to console
me.
“You do realize I’m not opposing them simply for the
sake of it. It’s been ages since I’ve put my powers to the
test,” I said, walking through the metallic vault and
stretching. “Well, it’s been a while since I took a leap,” I
added, pushing off and effortlessly reaching the top of the
spiral staircase. Upon landing, I heard another thud.
“You know, the staircase is rather useless,” I chuckled.
“Well, we do need to send some cleaning staff down
there as well,” the Irishman replied.
As I exited the ‘P.O.F’ building, my British driver
stood by the gate, ready to open my car. Everything was
as usual, and we drove in silence. However, the silence
was suddenly broken as the car came to a halt.
“Who dares stop me?” I questioned, stepping out of
the vehicle. To my surprise, the person before me was
none other than the father of my fallen friend, Jerry.
128
Threshold’s Pursuit
“Uncle, you shouldn’t have. You could have just
called me, and I would have come,” I said, attempting to
make amends for my earlier rudeness.
“Well, Charlie, there are a few things I’ve kept hidden
from Jerry that need to be revealed,” Jerry’s father
explained. “I am Défteros, the Fourth Hero of the First
World, second only to The Great ENUMA,” he
continued, sending a shiver down my spine.
“Wait, that means...” I began to say, but he cut me off.
“Yes, the Sixth Calamity Class must be Jerry, because
I used all the life force of the Angel of Creation to create
a fate where my family would forever be happy,” Défteros
admitted, his voice filled with melancholy. “I was dubbed
‘the greedy one’ by God and ended up establishing a
dictatorship,” I added, my voice devoid of mercy as I
remembered how his selfishness had caused suffering in
my kingdom in the First World.
“But I attended his funeral. Don’t reincarnated people
go into a coma in our world?” I inquired.
“Well, there’s always the second option of full-body
reincarnation,” Défteros explained. “So you faked a
funeral for Public, but then you saw a P.O.F. member
meeting me at the church. Quite lucky you got there, it
seems. I must have left my chrono Destinatus off for too
long.”
I spoke, but Jerry’s father quickly walked away. I, too,
had little desire to continue the conversation, and I
retreated to my car. “Step on it,” I commanded the driver,
and he accelerated. We soon arrived at my penthouse.
“Sir, your family seems to be waiting at the mansion,”
my driver informed me politely.
“Exactly why I chose this destination,” I replied. I
129
Teerth Sharma
quickly exited the car and entered the elevator, taking a
few moments to collect my thoughts.
“Now, I need to concentrate,” I reminded myself as I
settled into a yoga-like stance in the middle of my room.
I muttered, “I hope this works quickly; I have a business
meeting scheduled.” With that, I began the process of
slowly dismantling the thick mana barrier outlining our
universe. But before I could attune my frequency to the
realm between universes, the realm of God, I was
involuntarily pulled into it.
“This marks the third time,” a voice stated, but it
sounded as though the speaker was conversing through a
vacuum cleaner, the words oscillating and messy.
“Well, well, well, if it isn’t the chained Archangel of
Destruction,” I remarked, observing a white man with
wings as white as snow, standing in the middle of the
white empty room, his hands clasped in prayer-like
fashion.
“Not so chained, and not so Archangel of Destruction
anymore, after you shouldered all the burden assigned to
me,” the Archangel replied in a playfully passive-
aggressive tone.
“I couldn’t help but pity you, being chained for billions
of years,” I retorted mockingly.
“Well, this time will be the last time you see me
because, as the rule dictates, I must grant you some part
of my power, and you’ve taken nearly all the time when
you freed me. So this time, I will give you my all,” the
angel said, turning his gaze towards me. The red iris that
I grown familiar to, now appeared filled with sadness.
“No, I won’t accept that. I already have enough of
these abilities,” I replied with frustration. I had grown
130
Threshold’s Pursuit
tired of people sacrificing for me just to earn my gratitude.
“Let’s examine your abilities as this will be for our last
time. God’s Left Hand, granting you Universal
Destruction Authority, the power to control destruction on
a universal scale. Cosmic Deicide, a misfit from the Rule
of God, which allows you to eliminate all concept law and
even the divine. And the Chrono-Destinatus. These
powers you possess can surely annihilate anything
unfortunate enough to cross your path. But Father’s will
is absolute alas I need to give you all my existence all my
powers all my authority and My title,” the angel explained
his expression were neutral but his voice had a sense of
urgency, before transforming into dark light and
vanishing. Suddenly, everything became clear to me.
“So that’s how it is,” I muttered, finally understanding
why my life had been so dramatic, and I was not pleased.
“God, I know you’re here. Show yourself,” I demanded.
“Well, son, what do you require?” a voice resonated
from within me, sending tremors through my chest.
“No, I’m not having that not showing yourself thing
anymore. I want to face you,” I demanded.
“Son, if I enter any realm with more presence than
what I’ve allocated to you right now, that realm will
collapse,” the voice replied.
“You do realize that this also applies to me,” I pointed
out as I released my magic, unveiling the dominion of
destruction. Everything began to collapse, and even the
white surroundings tore apart, revealing darkness in its
wake.
“Well, you’ve made quite a mess,” a voice remarked
from behind me, suddenly restoring the arena.
“You do realize that was a mere fraction of the powers
131
Teerth Sharma
I have,” I retorted as I turned to face a white figure with
no face and no body, just a white outline. “What did you
want to say, son?” the voice grew sterner by the second.
“Well, you do know that you’re being controlled,” I
said as a vortex opened in front of me.
“LEAVE!” the god shouted.
To be continued…
132
ABOUT THE AUTHOR
Obviously talking in third Person is difficult for me
I am Teerth Sharma, Allen, 12th grader currently at 2023
and I enjoy many pursuit in life.
